claim: Epigenetic modulating agents (EMAs) modulate antitumor immune response in a cancer model system.

result.claim: Epigenetic modulating agents (EMAs) modulate antitumor immune response in a cancer model system.
result.reasoning:
The claim is strongly supported by evidence from multiple propositions. Paper ID 9767444, titled "Epigenetic therapy activates type I interferon signaling in murine ovarian cancer to reduce immunosuppression and tumor burden," provides direct evidence. It states that "clinically relevant doses of DNA methyltransferase and histone deacetylase inhibitors (DNMTi and HDACi, respectively) reduce the immune suppressive microenvironment through type I IFN signaling" (prop_992) in a mouse model of epithelial ovarian cancer. Furthermore, it details that AZA (a DNMTi) increases CD45+ immune cells (prop_994), active CD8+ T and natural killer (NK) cells (prop_995), and reduces macrophages and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (prop_999) in the tumor microenvironment. The paper also notes that adding an HDACi to AZA enhances this modulation (prop_1000).

Additionally, paper ID 9955779, "Epigenetic Therapy Ties MYC Depletion to Reversing Immune Evasion and Treating Lung Cancer," supports the claim by stating that "the combination of DNA-demethylating agents (DNMTis) and histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACis) holds promise for enhancing cancer immune therapy" (prop_1002) and that "the combination treatment schema reverses tumor immune evasion in mouse models of NSCLC" (prop_1008). Both papers explicitly refer to "mouse models," fulfilling the "cancer model system" criterion. The consistent findings across these studies, detailing the specific immune modulatory effects of EMAs, provide strong support for the claim.
